Native speakers say 'I gotta go' more than 'I have to leave'.
'Kinda' and 'sorta' are everywhere in casual speech. Don't sound formal.
Replace 'I want to know' with 'I'm curious about'. Sounds less demanding.
Drop 'very'. Use specific words: 'exhausted' beats 'very tired'.
Stress matters. 'I didn't say HE stole it' vs 'I didn't say he STOLE it' — different meanings.
'How's it going?' is a greeting, not a question. Answer 'Good, you?' and move on.
Use 'though' at the end of a sentence to soften: 'It's expensive though.'
Filler words are okay. 'You know', 'like', 'I mean' — they buy thinking time.
Reply to 'thanks' with 'no problem', 'sure thing', or 'you got it' — not 'you're welcome' every time.
Schwa /ə/ is the most common vowel. 'Banana' is 'bə-NAN-ə', not 'BAH-NAH-NAH'.
Contractions are required. 'I'm', 'you're', 'don't' — not 'I am', 'you are', 'do not'.
End rising intonation makes a statement sound like a question. Watch your tone.
'Catch up' has 10+ meanings. Context, not memorization.
Use 'actually' to correct gently. 'Actually, it's $20, not $15.'
American 'r' is bunched, not rolled. The tongue doesn't touch the roof.
'I'm down' = 'I'm in'. 'I'm out' = 'I'm not joining'. Confusing but standard.
Past tense 'd' is often a tap, not a hard d. 'Wanted' sounds like 'WAN-id'.
Stop apologizing in English. 'My bad' or 'oh sorry' once, then move on.
'How about you?' beats 'And you?' in casual conversation.
Natives often skip 'that': 'I think you're right' instead of 'I think that you're right'.
'Gonna' replaces 'going to' in casual speech. 'I'm gonna call him' sounds natural; 'I am going to call' sounds stiff.
'Wanna' replaces 'want to'. 'You wanna grab coffee?' beats 'Do you want to grab coffee?'
'Lemme' replaces 'let me'. 'Lemme check' is how natives speak; 'Let me check' is fine but formal.
'Gimme' replaces 'give me'. 'Gimme a sec' sounds natural in casual contexts.
'Dunno' replaces 'don't know'. 'I dunno' is everyday; 'I do not know' is too formal.
'Outta' replaces 'out of'. 'I'm outta milk' beats 'I am out of milk'.
'Coulda', 'shoulda', 'woulda' replace 'could have', 'should have', 'would have' in speech.
'Hafta' is how 'have to' actually sounds. 'I hafta go' is what you'll hear.
'Gotta' replaces 'got to' or 'have got to'. 'You gotta try this'.
'Tryna' replaces 'trying to'. 'I'm tryna finish this' is common in casual speech.
'Ya' replaces 'you' in fast speech. 'See ya later' beats 'See you later' in casual goodbyes.
'D'ya' replaces 'do you'. 'D'ya wanna go?' is common; full form sounds robotic.
'Whatcha' replaces 'what are you'. 'Whatcha doing?' is the natural casual version.
'Howdja' replaces 'how did you'. 'Howdja find out?' sounds native.
'Wouldja' replaces 'would you'. 'Wouldja mind?' is normal everyday speech.
'Couldja' replaces 'could you'. 'Couldja pass the salt?' beats the full form.
'Didja' replaces 'did you'. 'Didja see that?' is everyday speech.
'Betcha' replaces 'bet you'. 'I betcha he forgot' is casual American English.
'Cuz' replaces 'because' in casual speech. Write 'because', but say 'cuz' or 'cause'.
'Hey' is the most common American casual greeting. 'Hey, what's up?' beats 'Hello'.
'What's up?' isn't asking what's above you. Answer 'Not much, you?' and keep walking.
'How you doing?' loses the 'are'. Just say 'Good, you?' in return.
'What's new?' is a greeting. Safe answer: 'Not much' or 'Same old'.
'Long time no see' is fine, even though it's broken grammar. Natives say it all the time.
'Morning!' alone works. You don't need to say 'Good morning' every time.
'Hey there' is friendlier than 'Hello'. Use with people you already know.
'Yo' is fine among friends but too casual at work with people you just met.
'Take care' is a common goodbye. 'Take care, see you Monday' sounds native.
'Catch you later' is casual 'goodbye'. Friendlier than 'Goodbye'.
'Have a good one' is a friendly all-purpose sign-off. Works at coffee shops, with coworkers, anywhere.
'I'll let you go' politely ends a phone call without sounding abrupt.
'Alright, I should run' signals you're wrapping up without saying 'Goodbye' coldly.
End a chat with 'Anyway,' to pivot to leaving. 'Anyway, I gotta head out.'
'I'm not sure I agree' is softer than 'I disagree'. Keeps the conversation open.
'I see what you mean, but...' acknowledges first, disagrees second. Less confrontational.
'That's a fair point, though I think...' validates before pushing back.
'I hear you, but...' is a way to push back without dismissing.
'I'd push back on that a little' is professional disagreement without aggression.
'I'm not so sure about that' is a soft 'no' that doesn't slam the door shut.
'I could be wrong, but...' lets you disagree while staying humble.
'Maybe' as a soft 'no' is common. 'Maybe later' often means 'probably not'.
'That's interesting' often means 'I don't agree but I won't fight you'. Tone matters.
'Can you...' beats 'Could you possibly...' in everyday requests. Direct isn't rude in American English.
'I need this by Friday' is polite enough at work. You don't have to soften every request.
Skip 'I am sorry to bother you, but...' in emails. Just ask. Natives don't pile on apologies.
'Just checking in' is a polite professional nudge without sounding pushy.
'Quick question' before asking is friendly and efficient. Don't preface with long apologies.
'When you get a chance' softens a request without being weak. 'When you get a chance, can you review this?'
'No rush' at the end of a request signals it's not urgent without sounding meek.
'Heads up' is a friendly warning. 'Heads up, the meeting moved to 3'.
'Let me know' is the standard close for asks. Friendly, not demanding.
'Figure out' replaces 'determine' or 'resolve'. 'Let me figure it out' beats 'Let me determine it'.
'Find out' replaces 'discover' or 'ascertain'. 'I'll find out' sounds normal; 'I'll ascertain' sounds like a lawyer.
'Come up with' replaces 'devise' or 'create'. 'I came up with an idea' beats 'I devised an idea'.
'Run into' replaces 'encounter'. 'I ran into Bob' sounds native.
'Bump into' replaces 'meet by chance'. 'I bumped into him yesterday'.
'Bring up' replaces 'mention' or 'raise'. 'She brought up the budget' sounds native.
'Look into' replaces 'investigate'. 'I'll look into it' beats 'I'll investigate it' for casual work talk.
'Put off' replaces 'postpone'. 'Let's put it off' beats 'Let's postpone it'.
'Call off' replaces 'cancel'. 'They called off the meeting' sounds native.
'Set up' replaces 'arrange' or 'establish'. 'I'll set up a call' beats 'I'll arrange a call'.
'Drop off' replaces 'deliver'. 'I'll drop it off' beats 'I'll deliver it'.
'Pick up' replaces 'retrieve' or 'collect'. 'I'll pick it up' is the everyday version.
'Show up' replaces 'arrive' or 'appear'. 'He didn't show up' sounds native.
'Hang out' replaces 'spend time together'. 'Wanna hang out?' is normal.
'Hang on' replaces 'wait a moment'. 'Hang on a sec' is everyday speech.
'Go over' replaces 'review'. 'Let's go over the slides' is what people say in meetings.
'Work out' replaces 'resolve' or 'exercise'. 'It'll work out' is encouraging.
'Run by' replaces 'check with'. 'Let me run it by my boss' is everyday work speech.
'Get back to' replaces 'respond to later'. 'I'll get back to you' is the standard.
'Reach out' replaces 'contact'. 'I'll reach out to her' sounds modern; 'I'll contact her' sounds formal.
'Touch base' means 'briefly connect'. 'Let's touch base Friday' is business-casual.
'Loop in' replaces 'include in communication'. 'Loop me in on the thread' is native business English.
'Catch up' replaces 'update each other'. 'Let's catch up over coffee'.
'Wrap up' replaces 'finish'. 'Let me wrap this up' beats 'Let me complete this'.
'Knock out' replaces 'finish quickly'. 'I'll knock it out tonight'.
'Pull off' replaces 'succeed in something difficult'. 'You actually pulled it off'.
'Throw together' replaces 'assemble quickly'. 'I threw together a deck' is casual work-speak.
'Break down' replaces 'analyze'. 'Let me break it down for you' is the natural version.
'Sort out' replaces 'organize' or 'resolve'. 'I'll sort it out'.
'It's not rocket science' = 'It's not that hard'. Common dismissive idiom.
'Piece of cake' = 'very easy'. 'That test was a piece of cake'.
'A no-brainer' = 'an obvious choice'. 'Taking the offer is a no-brainer'.
'On the same page' = 'in agreement'. 'Just making sure we're on the same page'.
'Drop the ball' = 'fail to follow through'. 'Sorry, I dropped the ball on that email'.
'Ball is in your court' = 'your turn to act'. Common in negotiations.
'Touch and go' = 'uncertain'. 'It was touch and go for a while'.
'Up in the air' = 'undecided'. 'The plan's still up in the air'.
'Long story short' = 'to summarize'. Use before giving the short version of something.
'At the end of the day' = 'ultimately'. Overused, but everyone says it.
'Cut to the chase' = 'skip to the main point'. 'Let me cut to the chase'.
'Spill the beans' = 'reveal a secret'. 'Don't spill the beans yet'.
'On thin ice' = 'in a risky situation'. 'You're on thin ice with the boss'.
'In a nutshell' = 'briefly summarized'. 'In a nutshell, it didn't work'.
'Under the weather' = 'sick'. 'I'm feeling under the weather' is polite for 'sick'.
'Get the hang of' = 'learn how'. 'I'm getting the hang of it'.
'Hit the ground running' = 'start fast'. 'She hit the ground running on day one'.
'Bite the bullet' = 'do the unpleasant thing'. 'I'll just bite the bullet and call him'.
'Bear with me' = 'be patient with me'. 'Bear with me, this'll take a sec'.
'Read the room' = 'sense the mood'. Useful when someone's being tone-deaf.
'Throw under the bus' = 'blame unfairly'. 'He threw me under the bus in the meeting'.
'Go down the rabbit hole' = 'get deeply absorbed'. 'I went down a rabbit hole researching it'.
'Get a kick out of' = 'really enjoy'. 'He gets a kick out of dad jokes'.
'Hit the sack' = 'go to sleep'. 'I'm gonna hit the sack'.
'Crash' = 'fall asleep'. 'I crashed at 10 last night'.
'Pull an all-nighter' = 'stay up all night working'. Common student/work phrase.
'Call it a day' = 'stop for now'. 'Let's call it a day'.
'Take a rain check' = 'reschedule'. 'Can I take a rain check on dinner?'
'In Britain say 'lift'; in the US say 'elevator'. Same machine.
Brits say 'flat'; Americans say 'apartment'.
Brits say 'lorry'; Americans say 'truck'.
Brits say 'queue'; Americans say 'line'. 'Get in line' in the US.
Brits say 'pavement'; Americans say 'sidewalk'.
Brits say 'biscuit'; Americans say 'cookie'. American 'biscuit' is a fluffy bread.
Brits say 'chips'; Americans say 'fries'. Brit 'crisps' = American 'chips'.
Brits say 'rubbish'; Americans say 'trash' or 'garbage'.
Brits say 'holiday'; Americans say 'vacation'. 'Holiday' in the US means Christmas/Thanksgiving.
Brits say 'petrol'; Americans say 'gas'. 'Fill up the gas tank'.
Brits say 'autumn'; Americans say 'fall' more often. Both understood.
Brits say 'football'; Americans say 'soccer'. American 'football' is the helmet sport.
Brits say 'jumper'; Americans say 'sweater'.
Brits say 'trousers'; Americans say 'pants'. In the UK, 'pants' means underwear.
Brits say 'maths'; Americans say 'math' (no 's').
Brits say 'university'; Americans say 'college' for undergrad. 'I'm in college' means university.
Brits 'go to hospital'; Americans 'go to the hospital' (with 'the').
Brits say 'cheers' for 'thanks' and 'goodbye'. Americans use it mainly when drinking.
Brits say 'mate'; Americans say 'buddy', 'dude', or 'man'.
Brits say 'brilliant!' for 'great!'; Americans say 'awesome!' or 'sweet!'
'Water' in American English sounds like 'WAH-der'. The 't' becomes a 'd'-like flap.
'Better' sounds like 'BED-er'. The double-t becomes a quick flap.
'Butter' sounds like 'BUD-er'. Don't pronounce a hard British 't'.
'Twenty' often sounds like 'TWEN-ee'. The 't' drops entirely.
'Internet' sounds like 'INNER-net' in fast American speech. Both 't' sounds soften.
'Interview' often sounds like 'INNER-view'. The 't' drops after 'n'.
'Center' sounds like 'SEN-er'. The 't' disappears after 'n'.
'Wanted' sounds like 'WAN-ed' in fast speech. Drop the 't' after 'n'.
'Mountain' sounds like 'MOUN-en' with a glottal stop, not 'MOUN-tain'.
'Important' sounds like 'im-POR-en' with a glottal stop replacing the 't'.
'Sentence' often sounds like 'SEN-ens'. The 't' drops after 'n'.
'Probably' often sounds like 'PROB-ly'. The middle 'a' disappears.
'Comfortable' sounds like 'COMF-ter-bul', not 'com-for-TAY-bul'.
'Vegetable' is 'VEJ-ta-bul', three syllables, not four.
'Chocolate' is 'CHOC-lit', two syllables in fast speech.
'Family' is often 'FAM-ly', two syllables.
'Camera' is 'CAM-ra', two syllables.
'Different' is usually 'DIF-rent', not 'DIF-er-ent'.
'Interesting' is 'IN-tres-ting', three syllables, not four.
'Restaurant' is 'RES-tront', two syllables, in casual speech.
'Going to' as a future marker collapses to 'gonna'. As literal motion, keep 'going to'.
Unstressed 'a' usually sounds like 'uh' (schwa). 'About' is 'uh-BOUT', not 'AY-bout'.
Unstressed 'the' before consonants is 'thuh'. 'Thuh book', not 'thee book'.
Unstressed 'to' usually sounds like 'tuh'. 'I want tuh go' in fast speech.
Unstressed 'and' becomes 'n'. 'Salt 'n' pepper', 'rock 'n' roll'.
Unstressed 'for' becomes 'fer'. 'This is fer you'.
Unstressed 'of' becomes 'uh'. 'Cup uh coffee', not 'cup OF coffee'.
Unstressed 'or' becomes 'er'. 'Coffee er tea?'
Unstressed 'at' becomes 'uht'. 'Meet me uht the door'.
Unstressed 'can' becomes 'kn'. 'I kn do it'. Stressed 'CAN' means emphasis.
'Can' vs 'can't': in American English, 'can' is short ('kn'), 'can't' is long ('CANT').
'Him' often drops the 'h': 'Tell 'im I'll be there'.
'Her' often drops the 'h': 'Give 'er the keys'.
'His' often drops the 'h' between words: 'It's 'is fault'.
'Them' often becomes ''em'. 'Tell 'em to wait'.
'Have' as auxiliary drops the 'h': 'Should've' sounds like 'SHOOD-uv'.
'Did you' often blends to 'didja'. 'Didja eat yet?'
'Would you' blends to 'wouldja'. 'Wouldja help me?'
'Got you' blends to 'gotcha'. 'Gotcha, makes sense'.
'Got to' blends to 'gotta'. Already covered, but it's a blending pattern.
'What do you' often becomes 'whadaya'. 'Whadaya think?'
'OBject' (noun) vs 'obJECT' (verb). 'The object' is the thing; 'to object' is to disagree.
'PREsent' (noun/adj) vs 'preSENT' (verb). 'A present' is a gift; 'to present' is to show.
'REcord' (noun) vs 'reCORD' (verb). 'A record' is a file; 'to record' is to capture.
'CONtract' (noun) vs 'conTRACT' (verb). 'A contract' is an agreement; 'to contract' is to shrink.
'PROduce' (noun, vegetables) vs 'proDUCE' (verb, to make).
'PROject' (noun) vs 'proJECT' (verb). 'My project'; 'to project confidence'.
'CONduct' (noun, behavior) vs 'conDUCT' (verb, to lead).
'PERmit' (noun, the license) vs 'perMIT' (verb, to allow).
'INcrease' (noun, the amount) vs 'inCREASE' (verb, to grow).
'SUSpect' (noun, the person) vs 'susPECT' (verb, to think).
'REbel' (noun) vs 'reBEL' (verb).
'CONvict' (noun, prisoner) vs 'conVICT' (verb, to find guilty).
'INsult' (noun) vs 'inSULT' (verb).
Stress 'PHOtograph', 'phoTOGrapher', 'photoGRAPHic'. The stress shifts; vowels reduce.
'INformation' stresses on 'MA'. Not 'in-for-MAY-tion' but 'in-fer-MAY-shen'.
'PSYchology' stresses on 'CHOL'. The 'p' is silent.
'AmeriCAN' stresses on 'MER', not 'A-meri-can' equally. 'uh-MER-i-cn'.
Compound nouns stress the first word: 'GREENhouse', 'WHITEhouse', 'BLACKboard'.
Adjective + noun stress the noun: 'green HOUSE' (a house that's green) vs 'GREENhouse' (the structure).
'A WHITE house' is any white-colored house. 'The WHITE House' is where the president lives.
'BLACKbird' is a species; 'a black BIRD' is any dark-colored bird.
'I never SAID he stole money' vs 'I never said HE stole money'. Stress changes meaning entirely.
Stressing 'I' = it wasn't me. Stressing 'YOU' = it was you. English moves meaning with stress.
Don't stress every word equally. English compresses unstressed syllables. That's the rhythm.
Function words (the, of, to, a, and) almost never get stress. Content words (nouns, verbs) do.
'Going TO the STORE' stresses the destination, not 'GOing TO the STORE'.
'I'll' for 'I will' is standard, not lazy. 'I'll call you' beats 'I will call you'.
'You'll' for 'you will'. 'You'll love it' is what natives say.
'It's' for 'it is'. Saying 'it is' over and over sounds robotic.
'There's' for 'there is'. 'There's a problem' beats 'There is a problem'.
'Who's' for 'who is'. 'Who's coming?' is the natural form.
'That's' for 'that is'. 'That's fine' is standard.
'What's' for 'what is'. 'What's that?' is the everyday form.
'I've', 'you've', 'we've', 'they've' for 'have'. 'I've been there' beats 'I have been there'.
'Don't' not 'do not'. Saying 'do not' sounds like a warning sign, not speech.
'Won't' for 'will not'. 'I won't go' beats 'I will not go'.
'Can't' for 'cannot'. Even in writing, 'cannot' is one word, not 'can not'.
'Isn't', 'aren't', 'wasn't', 'weren't'. Negative contractions are default in speech.
'Shouldn't', 'wouldn't', 'couldn't'. Always contract modal negatives in speech.
Double contractions exist in speech: 'I'd've' for 'I would have'. Don't write it, but you'll hear it.
'You'd' covers both 'you would' and 'you had'. Context tells you which.
'Y'all' is Southern but widely understood. Friendly second-person plural.
'You guys' is the neutral American plural 'you'. Works for any gender mix in casual speech.
'Ain't' is non-standard but very common in speech and song. Avoid in formal writing.
'Excuse me' to get attention; 'I'm sorry' for apologies. Don't mix them.
'Sorry' alone is enough for small bumps. You don't need 'I am terribly sorry'.
'Real quick' before a request softens it. 'Real quick, can I ask you something?'
'Just' softens almost anything. 'I just wanted to ask' beats 'I wanted to ask'.
But don't overuse 'just' in emails. Women especially get told to drop it. Use sparingly.
'Kind of' (kinda) softens claims. 'It's kinda weird' is less confrontational than 'It's weird'.
'A bit' softens like 'kinda'. 'I'm a bit tired'.
'Pretty much' = 'almost entirely'. 'I'm pretty much done'.
'Basically' = 'in essence'. Used a lot, sometimes too much. 'Basically, we lost'.
'Honestly' signals you're being direct. 'Honestly, I didn't like it'.
'To be fair' acknowledges the other side. 'To be fair, he was tired'.
'No offense, but...' warns a critical comment is coming. Use sparingly.
'With all due respect' precedes disagreement with a superior. Formal but useful.
'I mean no disrespect, but...' is the same move, slightly softer.
'Look, ...' before an opinion signals seriousness. 'Look, I just don't think it'll work'.
'Listen, ...' is like 'Look' — signals you're about to say something important.
'See, here's the thing' sets up an explanation or pushback.
'The thing is...' introduces a complication. 'The thing is, I already promised her'.
'Here's the deal' introduces an explanation or offer. 'Here's the deal: I'll cover Friday'.
'Anyway' pivots topics or signals you're wrapping up. 'Anyway, what were you saying?'
'So...' starts a story or pivots a topic. Don't start emails with it, but in speech it's fine.
'Yeah, no' = polite 'no'. 'Yeah, no, I can't make it' sounds friendlier than a flat 'no'.
'No, yeah' = 'yes', emphasized. 'No, yeah, totally agree'.
'Totally' = strong agreement. 'Totally, I get it'.
'Definitely' = firm agreement. Stronger than 'sure'.
'Absolutely' = stronger 'yes'. 'Can I borrow this?' 'Absolutely'.
'A hundred percent' = full agreement. 'A hundred percent, you're right'.
Asking 'right?' at sentence-end checks agreement. 'It's cold today, right?'
'Huh?' is a polite 'I didn't catch that' in casual speech. Not rude unless tone is harsh.
'Say that again?' is friendlier than 'Repeat that'.
'Sorry, what was that?' is the polite request to repeat.
'Come again?' is a casual 'say that again' — friendly, not aggressive.
'You're kidding!' shows surprise. 'No way!' is similar.
'Get out!' (with rising tone) means 'I don't believe it', not 'leave'.
'Shut up!' (playful tone) means 'No way!' in surprise. Tone is everything.
'For real?' = 'Really?' Casual surprise. 'For real, he said that?'
Don't translate 'sumimasen' as 'I'm sorry' every time. Often the natural fit is 'excuse me' or 'thanks'.
Rising tone on yes/no questions: 'You coming?' goes up. Flat tone sounds robotic.
Falling tone on wh-questions: 'Where's the meeting?' goes down at the end, not up.
Statement-as-question works with rising tone alone: 'You're leaving?' instead of 'Are you leaving?'
List intonation: rise on each item, fall on the last. 'Coffee, tea, or water?' — last word drops.
Don't end statements with a rise — it sounds uncertain. 'I finished it.' goes down.
Tag questions flip meaning by tone: 'You're coming, right?' rising = real question, falling = expecting yes.
Stress 'really' with a high pitch to show genuine surprise: 'Really?!' not flat 'really.'
'Oh' carries meaning by pitch alone. Flat 'oh' = neutral, rising 'oh?' = curious, falling 'oh.' = disappointed.
Borrow = take from someone. Lend = give to someone. 'Can I borrow your pen?' not 'Can you borrow me your pen?'
Bring = toward speaker. Take = away from speaker. 'Bring it here' vs 'Take it there.'
Say = words spoken. Tell = inform a person. 'He said hi' but 'He told me hi' is wrong — say 'He told me he said hi.'
'Tell' needs a person: 'tell me, tell him.' 'Say' doesn't: 'say something.'
Make vs do: 'make a decision, make a mistake, do homework, do the dishes.' Memorize collocations.
Win vs beat: you win a game, you beat a person. 'I beat him at chess' not 'I won him.'
Hear vs listen: hear is passive, listen is active. 'I heard a noise' vs 'I'm listening to music.'
See vs watch vs look: see happens to you, watch is sustained attention, look is directed glance.
Remember vs remind: I remember (myself), you remind me (someone else).
Fun vs funny: fun = enjoyable, funny = makes you laugh (or weird). 'The party was fun', not 'funny.'
Sensitive vs sensible: sensitive = emotional, sensible = practical. Don't call someone 'sensible' meaning kind.
Sympathy vs empathy: sympathy = feel sorry for, empathy = feel with. Different.
Economic vs economical: economic = about the economy, economical = cheap to run.
Historic = important in history. Historical = related to history. Say 'historic moment', not 'historical moment.'
Classic vs classical: classic = timeless example, classical = the old art/music tradition.
Lose vs loose: lose = misplace, loose = not tight. 'I lose my keys, my pants are loose.'
Few = almost none (sad). A few = some (normal). 'I have few friends' sounds lonely; 'I have a few' is fine.
Little vs a little: same pattern. 'A little tired' = somewhat. 'Little tired' = barely.
Especially vs specially: especially = above all, specially = for a special purpose.
Small talk opener: 'How's your week going?' Beats 'How are you?' — gives them something specific to answer.
Friday opener: 'Got any plans for the weekend?' Monday: 'How was your weekend?'
Weather works in the US too: 'Crazy weather we're having, huh?' Don't dismiss it as too basic.
Compliment the specific: 'I love your sneakers' beats generic 'You look nice.'
Ask about their drink/food at events: 'What are you drinking? Is it good?' Easy entry.
'What brings you here?' is the standard event-mingle opener. Use it freely.
Reference shared context: 'How do you know [host]?' at parties. Instant common ground.
End small talk cleanly: 'I'm gonna grab another drink — good talking to you.' No awkward fade.
'I'll let you go' is the polite phone/hallway exit. Other person feels respected, not abandoned.
'It was good seeing you' as a farewell. Past tense even though they're still standing there.
Don't answer 'How are you?' literally. 'Good, you?' is the ritual, not a real question.
'Long time no see' is fine, not broken English. Native speakers say it.
'What's new?' as a follow-up after greetings. Open-ended, lets them pick the topic.
'Up to anything fun tonight?' is the after-work casual ask. Don't overthink it.
If small talk dies, blame yourself out loud: 'Anyway...' then pivot. Buys you a reset.
'Sync' = short meeting to align. 'Let's sync tomorrow at 2.' Sounds way more native than 'meeting.'
'Catch up' = informal 1:1 to update each other. 'Wanna catch up Friday?' for both work and friends.
'Circle back' = revisit later. 'Let's circle back on this next week.' Slightly corporate but standard.
'Touch base' = brief check-in. 'I'll touch base with him.' Don't overuse — it's a tic.
'Loop me in' = include me on the thread. 'Loop me in when you hear back.'
'Out of pocket' in US workplaces means unavailable, not paying yourself. Causes confusion with non-US folks.
'EOD' = end of day, 'EOW' = end of week. Drop the acronyms in Slack like everyone else.
'Heads up' = advance warning. 'Just a heads up, the demo is at 3.' Use it constantly.
'Quick question' before asking. Signals you won't take much time, even if you will.
'Hop on a call' beats 'have a meeting on the phone.' Standard US workplace verb.
'Jump in' = interrupt politely. 'Can I jump in here?' during a Zoom is normal.
'Take this offline' = stop discussing in the meeting, talk later. 'Let's take this offline.'
'Bandwidth' = capacity. 'I don't have the bandwidth this week.' Polite no.
'On my plate' = workload. 'I've got a lot on my plate right now.'
'Drop a line' = send a quick message. 'Drop me a line when you're free.'
'Ping me' = message me. 'Ping me on Slack when you're done.' Standard tech-office verb.
'No rush' after a request softens the deadline. 'Can you review this? No rush.'
'When you get a chance' = polite, non-urgent. Pair with the ask: 'When you get a chance, take a look.'
'Sounds good' is the workplace yes. Use it 10x a day, nobody minds.
'Works for me' = I agree with the plan. 'Tuesday at 3 works for me.'
'I'm down' = I'm in. 'Wanna grab lunch?' 'I'm down.' Casual but office-acceptable.
'Game' means willing. 'I'm game' = sure, let's do it.
'Wanna' instead of 'do you want to' in casual office talk. 'Wanna grab coffee?'
'Sorry, could you repeat that?' beats 'What?' which sounds rude in the US.
'I didn't catch that' is the smoothest 'I didn't understand.' Sounds native, not non-native.
'Say that again?' with rising tone = please repeat. Don't say 'Pardon?' — too formal in the US.
'What was the last part?' is better than 'Repeat everything.' Shows you got most of it.
'Sorry, you lost me' = I stopped following. Honest and friendly.
'Wait, back up' = ask them to restart. Casual, not rude.
'How do you mean?' instead of 'What do you mean?' Sounds softer and curious, not confrontational.
'Just to clarify...' before restating their point. Looks engaged, not confused.
'Let me make sure I got that...' then paraphrase. Professional clarification move.
'Could you spell that?' for names. No shame, everyone asks.
'Sorry, the accent threw me off' is fine to say. People appreciate the honesty.
'One more time, slower?' for fast speakers. Specific ask gets specific result.
Ask for the meaning, not the word: 'What does [X] mean in this context?' shows nuance.
'I'm not familiar with that term' beats 'I don't know that word.' Sounds more grown-up.
'Run that by me again?' = please re-explain. Native phrasing.
'You mean [X], right?' confirms your guess. Faster than asking blind.
'Sorry, did you say [X] or [Y]?' is the cleanest minimal-pair check.
'Totally' as agreement is everywhere in the US. 'You think it's too hot?' 'Totally.'
'For sure' = strong yes. 'You coming?' 'For sure.'
'100%' as agreement. 'Best taco in the city.' '100%.' Younger register but widespread.
'Absolutely' is emphatic but slightly formal. Good for business yes.
'You bet' = enthusiastic yes, especially in the western US. 'Need a hand?' 'You bet.'
'No doubt' = I agree completely. Don't confuse with 'doubtful.'
'Right?!' rising-falling tone agrees with what someone just said. 'It's so cold.' 'Right?!'
'I know, right?' is the classic 'I totally agree' phrase. Use it constantly.
'Couldn't agree more' for stronger workplace agreement.
'Same here' = me too, in a casual register. 'I love that show.' 'Same here.'
'Hard same' is Gen-Z amplifier of 'same.' Use with younger crowd only.
'Preach' = strong agreement (originally Black American English, now mainstream). Read the room.
'Facts' = that's true. 'Traffic is brutal.' 'Facts.' Casual, young.
'You're not wrong' = soft agreement, often after a complaint. 'This place is overpriced.' 'You're not wrong.'
'Hell yeah' for friends, not the office. Strong enthusiasm.
Disagree softly with 'I see what you mean, but...' rather than 'No.'
'I'm not sure I agree' is more polite than 'I disagree.'
'I'd push back on that a little' = I disagree, professional version. Standard in US tech.
'That's one way to look at it, but...' validates them before pivoting.
'I hear you, but...' acknowledges before disagreeing.
'I'm gonna have to disagree there' uses softening 'gonna have to' to lighten it.
'Respectfully, I think...' is the workplace template for hard disagreement.
'Hmm, I don't know about that' is a soft 'I disagree' without saying it.
'I'm not so sure' = polite doubt. Less harsh than 'I don't think so.'
'Eh, I dunno' with a head tilt = soft pushback. Body language matters.
'I see it a bit differently' is the cleanest professional disagree.
Avoid bare 'No, you're wrong.' Even when true, it kills the conversation in US workplaces.
'Fair point, but...' acknowledges before contradicting. Keeps the other person open.
'I take your point, but my concern is...' professional disagreement template.
'Let's agree to disagree' to exit gracefully when neither side will budge.
'Bummer' = that's too bad. 'My flight got canceled.' 'Oh, bummer.' Casual sympathy.
'Sucks' = is bad. 'Mondays suck.' Mild, not rude. Don't avoid it like a swear.
'Lit' = exciting/great. Mostly younger speakers, slightly dated now but still used.
'Vibe' as noun = feeling/atmosphere. 'The place has a cool vibe.'
'Vibe' as verb = enjoy/fit with. 'I'm vibing with this song.'
'Hits different' = stands out positively. 'This coffee hits different.'
'Lowkey' = kind of, secretly. 'I lowkey want to leave early.' Common in mixed company.
'Highkey' = openly, very. 'I'm highkey obsessed with this show.'
'No cap' = no lie. Younger; skip in older mixed company.
'It's giving [X]' = it has the feel of X. 'It's giving 90s.' Newer slang, trendy.
'Slaps' = is really good (usually food/music). 'This pizza slaps.'
'Sus' = suspicious. Mainstream now but feels casual.
'Ghost' = stop replying suddenly. 'He ghosted me' is standard everywhere now.
'Salty' = bitter/annoyed. 'Don't be salty.' Mild, mixed-company safe.
'Shade' = subtle insult. 'Throwing shade.' Mainstream.
'Flex' = show off. 'Nice flex.' Often sarcastic.
'Crash' = sleep. 'I'm gonna crash.' Useful and universal.
'Hang' as verb = casually spend time. 'Wanna hang Sunday?'
'Chill' as verb/adjective. 'Let's just chill' / 'He's super chill.'
'Wild' = crazy/surprising. 'That's wild.' Replaces 'crazy' without sounding harsh.
'Sick' = awesome (informal). Context-dependent; tone tells you which.
Order coffee: 'Can I get a [size] [drink]?' not 'I want.' 'Can I get' is the US default.
Sizes: tall = small, grande = medium, venti = large at Starbucks. Confusing but learn it.
'For here or to go?' is the standard question. 'For here' = eat in, 'to go' = takeaway.
'Room for cream?' = should I leave space at the top. Just say 'yes' or 'no, fill it up.'
Order milk: 'with oat milk' / 'almond milk' / 'whole milk' — specify or you get whole.
Order eggs: 'over easy' (runny yolk, flipped), 'sunny side up' (not flipped), 'scrambled.' Learn the menu of styles.
'I'll have the [X]' is the smoothest restaurant order. Beats 'I want.'
'Could I get the [X]?' is the polite version. Server-friendly.
'Just water for me, thanks' to skip ordering a drink without explaining.
'What do you recommend?' to crowdsource the order. Servers like the question.
'What's good here?' is casual version of 'recommend.' Use at diners and casual spots.
'How's the [dish]?' asks the server's opinion on one item. Specific, easy to answer.
'Can I do [X] instead of [Y]?' to substitute. 'Can I do salad instead of fries?'
'Hold the [X]' = without X. 'Hold the onions.' Standard.
'On the side' = serve separately. 'Dressing on the side.'
'Make it a double' for stronger drink/larger size.
Tipping isn't optional in the US. 18-22% is standard at sit-down restaurants.
Don't say 'check, please' — wave gently or catch eye. 'Could we get the check whenever you're ready?'
'Split the check' = separate bills. 'Can we split the check?' Servers can usually do it.
'Going Dutch' = each pay own. Dated but understood.
'It's on me' = I'm paying. 'Don't worry about it, it's on me.'
'Cheers' before drinking — standard in the US too now, not just UK.
'How is everything?' from server mid-meal = just say 'great, thanks' unless something's actually wrong.
'Still working on it?' from server = are you done. Say 'Still going' or 'I'm done, you can take it.'
'To stay' or 'to go?' at cafes — same as 'for here or to go.'
'Make it extra hot' for very hot coffee (Starbucks specifically).
'Light ice' = less ice. 'No ice' = none. 'Easy on the [X]' = less of it.
Bar order: 'Can I get a [drink]?' Don't say 'please' twice — once is enough or feels forced.
'Open a tab' = keep drinks on one bill. 'Should I open a tab?' bartender will ask.
'Close out' = pay the tab. 'I'm gonna close out.'
Order pizza by slice: 'A slice of pepperoni, please.' Don't say 'one piece.'
'Combo' = drink + side included. 'Make it a combo' adds them.
Checkout opener from cashier: 'Did you find everything okay?' Just say 'Yeah, thanks.'
'Paper or plastic?' (bagging) — answer with the word you want.
'Cash or card?' — 'Card, please.'
'Credit or debit?' — answer the type. They might also ask 'debit?' to confirm.
'Do you want the receipt?' — 'No, I'm good' or 'Yeah, thanks.'
'Are you a member?' = loyalty card. Just say 'No, thanks' unless you are.
'Phone number for our rewards?' — 'I'm good, thanks' is the polite skip.
'Donate a dollar to [charity]?' — 'Not today, thanks' is the polite no.
'Need a bag?' — 'No, I'm good' or 'Yes, please.'
'How are you paying?' — 'Card' / 'Cash' / 'Apple Pay.' Short answer is fine.
Tap to pay = 'I'll just tap.' Don't overexplain.
'Have a good one' from cashier — reply 'You too' even if nonsensical.
'Take care' as a farewell at stores. Same energy as 'goodbye.'
If declined, say 'Let me try another card' — staying calm helps the awkwardness.
Returns: 'I'd like to return this' + receipt. 'Refund' is the noun, 'return' is what you do.
'Do you have this in a [size/color]?' for clothing. Standard ask.
'Where would I find [X]?' at big stores. 'Find' beats 'is.'
'Just looking, thanks' to dismiss salesperson without being rude.
'I'll think about it' = no but politely. Used constantly to exit pitches.
Self-checkout: stay calm when it errors. Wave the attendant over — they're used to it.
Price match: 'They have it cheaper at [store]. Do you price match?' Direct works.
'Is this on sale?' beats decoding signs. Cashier knows.
'Can I get a raincheck?' = note that I'll buy it later at sale price when restocked. US-specific.
'Cash back?' at debit checkout = withdraw cash on the transaction. Say yes/no clearly.
End the question with rising tone if you genuinely don't know: 'You finished it?' rising = real ask.
End with falling tone if you're stating something you already know: 'You finished it.' falling = confirmation.
Sarcasm uses flat or downward tone on positive words: 'Great.' (falling) = it's not great.
Exaggerated rising tone on 'really' shows disbelief: 'Reeeally?'
Stress shifts meaning: 'I didn't say HE stole it' (someone else did) vs 'I didn't SAY he stole it' (I implied).
Long vowel + falling tone on 'oh' = realization. 'Ohhh, I get it.'
'Yes' with falling tone = neutral. 'Yes?' rising = answering a knock or a call.
Don't apologize before disagreeing every time. 'Hmm, I see it differently' is enough.
Use 'might' to soften statements: 'You might want to check that' is softer than 'You should check that.'
'Just' as a softener: 'Just wanted to ask...' softer than 'I wanted to ask.'
End some sentences with 'or something': 'Maybe coffee or something.' Sounds casual and open.
End with 'I guess' to hedge: 'It's fine, I guess.' Tentative agreement.
'Yeah no' = polite no. 'No yeah' = polite yes. Confusing but very American — listen for the final word.
'For what it's worth...' before an opinion makes it gentler.
'Just my two cents' after an opinion downplays it.
'I could be wrong, but...' lets you state strong opinions without locking in.
Borrow money 'from' someone. Never 'borrow with' or 'borrow to.'
Lend money 'to' someone. 'Can you lend me $20?' or 'Can you lend $20 to me?'
'Loan' as a verb is fine in the US: 'Can you loan me $20?' Brits dislike it; Americans don't care.
'Bring' it 'to' a place where listener is. 'Take' it 'to' a place neither of you is at.
Order tea: 'Hot tea' to distinguish from iced. In the South, 'tea' alone defaults to iced sweet tea.
'Iced coffee' vs 'cold brew' — different drinks. Cold brew is steeped cold, smoother.
Coffee size confusion: 'small' / 'medium' / 'large' works at most non-Starbucks places.
'Black coffee' = no milk, no sugar. Don't say 'just coffee' — ambiguous.
'Cream and sugar?' at diners = how do you take it. Answer with what you want.
'Refill?' on coffee at US diners is often free. Say 'yes, please' or 'I'm good.'
'Splash of milk' = small amount. 'Just a splash, thanks.'
'Light and sweet' (NYC deli) = lots of milk, lots of sugar. Order it once for the experience.
Order pizza toppings 'on half': 'Half pepperoni, half plain.'
Order eggs with 'whites only' if you don't want yolks. Common in the US.
'Easy on the [X]' = light. 'Easy on the salt.'
'Side of [X]' = order X separately. 'Side of ranch.'
'Double up on [X]' = extra of X. 'Double up on the bacon.'
Server greeting 'How are you guys doing tonight?' — 'Good, thanks' is enough.
Server: 'Can I start you off with drinks?' — 'Just water for now' if you need a menu minute.
'Are you ready to order, or do you need a minute?' — 'A minute, please' is fine and common.
When food arrives: 'Thank you' / 'Looks great' / 'Thanks so much.' Small acknowledgment matters.
End of meal: 'That was great, thank you' to server, even briefly.
'Compliments to the chef' is fine but sounds slightly formal. 'Tell the chef that was amazing' is more natural.
Coffee order template: 'Can I get a [size] [milk] [drink], please?' Stick to the order: size, milk, drink.
'Decaf' if you want no caffeine. Don't say 'no caffeine' — they'll be confused.
'Pour over' = slow-drip method. Know the word at third-wave shops.
'Macchiato' at Starbucks ≠ macchiato anywhere else. Starbucks one is sweeter. Heads up.
Tap to confirm tip on the screen: 18 / 20 / 25 / custom. Pick or hit custom.
'No tip' button often hidden. Just hit 'custom' and enter 0 if needed.
Coffee shop tipping: $1-2 per drink is normal. Card tip works too.
Bartender tip: $1 per drink or 20% on tab. Strict rule.
Uber/Lyft tip: 15-20% on the app after ride. Easy to forget.
Hairdresser tip: 20%, more for great service. Bring cash if possible.
Hotel housekeeping: $3-5/day in cash on the pillow. Many people forget.
'After you' at doors/elevators. Hold doors longer than feels natural — Americans expect it.
Eye contact + smile at strangers in the US in passing. In Japan it's odd, here it's basic politeness.
'Excuse me' (rising tone) to get attention. 'Excuse me' (falling) when squeezing past.
If someone sneezes near you, 'Bless you' is standard. Even strangers say it.
If someone holds a door, say 'thanks' even mid-sprint. Skipping it is rude.
'Sorry' when bumping into someone, even slightly. Both people say it.
Tip the pizza delivery driver $5 minimum or 15-20%, whichever is more.
'Is this seat taken?' at coffee shops if pulling up a chair from another table.
'Mind if I sit here?' (rising) when sharing a long table. Wait for the nod.
Asking time: 'Got the time?' or 'Do you have the time?' Sounds native.
Asking directions: 'Excuse me, do you know how to get to [X]?' beats 'Where is X?'
Drop 'Really?' for surprise. Try 'Wait, really?' or just 'Wait, what?' — sounds way more native.
'No way' beats 'Really?' for genuine shock. Use it when someone tells you something wild.
'Shut up!' as surprise is friendly, not rude. 'You got the job? Shut up!' reads as excited.
'Are you serious?' lands harder than 'really?' when you actually can't believe it.
'Get out!' means 'no way' in surprise contexts. 'You met him? Get out!'
Drop 'Oh my God' to two syllables: 'omigod' — full enunciation sounds dramatic.
'You're kidding me' for medium surprise. 'Are you kidding me?' is stronger, often annoyed.
'Hold on' before reacting buys you a beat. 'Hold on — she said what?'
'Wait wait wait' (3x) signals 'I need to process this.' Native rhythm, not stiff.
'No kidding?' is mild surprise + interest. Lower stakes than 'no way'.
'Huh' (flat tone) means mild interest. 'Huh!' (rising) means 'I didn't know that.'
'For real?' is casual surprise-check. Don't say 'Is it true?' — too literal.
'Whoa' (drawn out) for genuine awe. 'Whooa, that's a big house.'
'Damn' as surprise is mild now. 'Damn, that's expensive.' Fine in casual settings.
'Holy cow' / 'holy crap' if you avoid swearing. Same intensity as stronger words.
'That's wild' for surprising stories. Don't say 'That's strange' — sounds judgy.
'That's crazy' is the default surprise reaction. Use it liberally — Americans do.
'I can't believe it' sounds stiff alone. Pair it: 'I literally can't believe it.'
'Seriously?!' with rising intonation = surprise. Flat 'seriously' = annoyed. Tone matters.
'You did NOT' (stressed NOT) means 'I'm shocked you did.' Not a literal denial.
'Stop it' as surprise: 'You won? Stop it!' — means 'I can't handle this news.'
'Since when?' captures surprise + question. 'Since when do you run marathons?'
'How is that even possible?' sounds natural. 'How can it be?' sounds translated.
'What the heck' for surprise + confusion. Safer than 'what the hell' at work.
'I had no idea' for backdated surprise. 'I had no idea you played guitar.'
'Wow' alone is fine but pair it: 'Wow, okay' or 'Wow, that's a lot.'
Don't say 'I'm surprised' flatly. Show it: 'Wait, hold up — really?'
'Whaaat' (drawn out) sounds curious-surprised. 'What' (clipped) sounds confrontational.
'That's news to me' = polite surprise + 'nobody told me.' Useful at work.
'I didn't see that coming' for surprise at outcomes, not facts.
'Plot twist' jokingly signals unexpected turns. 'Plot twist: he was the boss.'
'Out of nowhere' for sudden events. 'She quit out of nowhere.'
'All of a sudden' beats 'suddenly' in speech. More natural rhythm.
When someone shares bad news, lead with 'Oh no' — not 'Really?'. 'Really?' sounds skeptical.
'I'm so sorry to hear that' is the safe default for sad news. Memorize this exact phrase.
Add 'that's awful' or 'that's rough' after 'I'm sorry' — keeps it from sounding rote.
Don't say 'I sympathize with you' — too formal. Say 'that sucks' or 'that's the worst.'
'Are you okay?' after bad news, even if obviously not. It's the gesture, not the question.
'Hang in there' = 'keep going, I'm rooting for you.' Standard comfort phrase.
Don't say 'Please cheer up' — sounds like a command. Say 'I hope you feel better soon.'
'That's a lot to deal with' validates without minimizing. Use it when someone's overwhelmed.
'I can't imagine' acknowledges you don't get it but care. Better than fake empathy.
'Take your time' when someone's processing. Not 'please be quick.'
'Whatever you need' offers help without specifics. Low-commitment, high-warmth.
Don't say 'Don't worry about it' to grief — sounds dismissive. Save it for small mistakes.
'Sending love' / 'thinking of you' in texts. Sounds natural; 'I pray for you' sounds heavy.
'Let me know if I can help' is polite but vague. 'I can bring dinner Thursday' is real help.
'You'll get through this' beats 'don't be sad.' Acknowledges struggle, predicts strength.
'I'm here if you want to talk' offers without forcing. Don't say 'tell me everything.'
When a pet dies, 'I'm so sorry' works. Don't say 'it's just a pet.'
For job loss: 'That's brutal, you didn't deserve that.' Validates first, advice never.
'Ugh, that's the worst' for minor venting. Don't over-formalize small annoyances.
'Oof' as a sympathy noise is fine. 'Oof, that's rough.' Sounds genuine.
Don't lecture or fix when someone vents. 'Yeah, that sucks' is often all they want.
'I've been there' shows shared experience. Only use if true — natives sniff out fake.
'It's okay to feel that way' validates emotions. Better than 'don't feel that way.'
Texting condolences: 'No need to reply, just thinking of you.' Removes obligation.
'Big hug' in text. 'Sending a hug' in voice. Don't physically hug coworkers unprompted.
'That's heavy' for emotionally weighty news. Not 'that's serious' — sounds clinical.
When someone's sick: 'Feel better soon' (casual) or 'get well soon' (card-formal).
Don't say 'do your best' to someone struggling. It pressures. Say 'you got this' instead.
'I really appreciate it' beats 'thank you very much' — sounds less formal-textbook.
'Thanks a ton' / 'thanks a million' for casual emphasis. Don't say 'thank you so much' every time.
'You're a lifesaver' when someone bails you out. Stronger than 'thanks.'
'I owe you one' creates warmth + future reciprocity. Don't say 'I have a debt to you.'
'That means a lot' for emotional thanks. Use when someone's words / gesture hit deep.
'You didn't have to' acknowledges generosity. Pair with 'but thank you' to accept gracefully.
Don't bow in your voice — just say 'thanks!' once and move on. Repeated thanks sounds anxious.
'Appreciate it' (dropped 'I') is super casual. Fine with coworkers, peers.
'Thanks for the heads up' when someone warns you. Standard idiom; learn it.
'Cheers' as thanks works in casual email signoffs. American-adjacent, not just British anymore.
'Much appreciated' is short, professional, warm. Good email closer after a request.
'You're the best' for friends doing favors. Light, warm, not romantic.
Reply to thanks with 'of course' or 'anytime' — sounds warmer than 'you're welcome.'
'Don't mention it' downplays a favor. Use when you genuinely don't want thanks.
'Happy to help' is the safe pro response to gratitude. Email-friendly.
'Glad I could help' is past tense. Use after the help is done, not during.
Acknowledge effort, not just outcome: 'Thanks for taking the time to look into this.'
Specific thanks lands better. 'Thanks for the doc' beats 'Thanks for everything.'
'I can't thank you enough' for big favors. Save for genuine moments — overuse dilutes.
'You went above and beyond' is professional praise that sounds human.
Don't say 'thank you for your kindness' — translated from JP. Say 'thanks for being cool about it.'
Soften 'no' with 'unfortunately' up front: 'Unfortunately I can't make it.'
'Would you mind...' is softer than 'Can you...'. Use for asks that interrupt.
'Whenever you get a chance' removes time pressure. Add to requests for non-urgent things.
'No rush' at the end of a request. Don't say 'please be quick' even if you want speed.
Add 'if possible' to soften: 'Could you send it by Friday, if possible?'
'Just checking in' for follow-ups. Beats 'why haven't you replied?' which sounds aggressive.
'Sorry to bug you' before interrupting. Not 'sorry to disturb' — too formal.
'When you have a sec' for tiny asks. Shortens 'second' on purpose, sounds light.
'Any chance you could...' is soft + concrete. 'Any chance you could review this today?'
'I was wondering if...' opens a soft request. 'I was wondering if you could help with X.'
Drop 'please' at start, add at end: 'Send me the file, please.' Sounds less commanding.
'If it's not too much trouble' is genuine softener, not just filler.
'Would it be possible to...' is formal-soft. Good for asks to senior people.
'Just a heads up' before delivering inconvenient info. Don't drop bombs cold.
'I hate to ask, but...' acknowledges the ask is a burden. Disarms resistance.
End an ask with '...if that works for you.' Gives recipient an out.
'Could I trouble you for...' is old-school polite. Use sparingly; sounds Victorian if overused.
Don't translate 'osewa ni narimasu' literally. Just say 'thanks again' or skip it.
Avoid 'please do' as a standalone — sounds Indian-English. Say 'go ahead' or 'sure.'
Soften disagreement-as-request: 'Could we maybe try X instead?' Not 'You should do X.'
'Mind if I...' = quick permission ask. 'Mind if I grab this seat?' Shorter than 'do you mind.'
Add 'real quick' to make an ask feel small: 'Can I ask you something real quick?'
Phone/Zoom signoff: 'I'll let you go' = polite way to end. Not 'I will hang up now.'
'Take care' is the default warm goodbye. Beats 'goodbye' which sounds flat / final.
'Have a good one' covers day/night/weekend. Lazy native default — use it.
'Catch you later' is friend-casual. Don't say it to your CEO.
'See you around' for vague future. Don't say 'see you next time' — translated.
'Talk soon' implies you'll actually talk soon. Don't say it as filler if you won't.
End emails with 'Best,' or 'Thanks,' — not 'Sincerely yours,' which is letter-formal.
'Cheers,' as email closer works in casual professional contexts. Try it.
Don't sign emails 'Regards' if you can avoid it — sounds cold-distant. 'Best' is warmer.
'Alright, I gotta run' to exit a conversation gracefully. Don't just disappear.
'I'll let you get back to it' = nice way to leave someone working.
'Don't be a stranger' = 'stay in touch.' Use with people you actually want to see again.
End calls with 'okay, bye-bye' (doubled). Single 'bye' can sound abrupt.
'Talk to you later' (TTYL) — fine spoken, dated in text. Just say 'talk soon.'
Don't say 'goodbye' in casual contexts. It's heavy, almost final. Use 'see ya.'
'Until next time' is fine in writing, stiff in speech. Skip it when talking.
Walking away from a chat: 'Anyway, good seeing you!' — gives a clean exit reason.
'I should probably head out' softens the leaving. Better than 'I will leave now.'
'Let's grab coffee sometime' is a vague friendly close. Both sides know it may not happen.
'Safe travels' before someone leaves on a trip. Don't say 'be careful' — sounds worried.
'Get home safe' after a night out. Standard caring close among friends.
Don't write 'thank you for your continued cooperation' — JP business translation. Just 'thanks!'
'Looking forward to it' as a closer if there's a next meeting planned. Sounds eager + warm.
Start anecdotes with 'So...' — buys time, signals story incoming. 'So, last week...'
'Okay so get this' = 'listen to this surprising thing.' Native storytelling opener.
'You know what happened?' invites the listener in. Better than just dumping the story.
'The funniest thing happened' previews the tone. Helps the listener react properly.
Open with the punchline-adjacent fact: 'I almost got fired yesterday.' Then back up.
Use present tense for past stories: 'So I'm walking to the store and this guy comes up...'
'Long story short' lets you skip details. Use when you sense the listener wants the point.
'To make a long story short' is the full version. Same meaning, slightly more formal.
'Anyway' resets a tangent. 'Anyway, so we got there and...' Common storytelling pivot.
'And get this' before a surprise twist. 'And get this — he was her ex.'
'You won't believe what happened' raises expectations. Make sure the payoff matches.
'I gotta tell you about...' is informal anecdote opener. Builds anticipation.
Drop 'Once upon a time' — fairy tale only. Real stories start with 'So, the other day...'
'Picture this' for vivid setups. 'Picture this: it's 2am, I'm in a Walmart parking lot.'
End anecdotes with a reaction beat: 'I just stood there.' Don't always need a moral.
'And I was like...' before quoting yourself. Standard American storytelling.
'And he was like...' for quoting others. Replaces 'he said.' Casual; don't use in writing.
Set the scene fast: time + place + who. 'Tuesday, in line at Trader Joe's, behind this guy...'
'No joke' / 'I swear' to flag unbelievable parts. 'No joke, the dog spoke English.'
'Speaking of which' transitions from someone else's topic to your story. Smooth.
'That reminds me of...' to piggyback on another's story. Better than abrupt topic-switch.
Don't preface stories with 'I have a funny story.' Just tell it. Self-billing kills the joke.
'Tasty' is fine but bland. Try 'incredible', 'unreal', 'addictive', 'so good' for food praise.
Don't say 'delicious' constantly — sounds textbook. 'So good' is the native default.
Describe food by feel: 'crispy', 'creamy', 'chewy', 'crunchy', 'flaky' — texture beats taste alone.
'Rich' = heavy/buttery/fatty. 'This sauce is rich.' Not 'this sauce is wealthy.'
'Light' as food praise = not heavy. 'The salad is light.' Compliment for healthy/refreshing.
'Refreshing' for drinks. 'This lemonade is so refreshing.' Better than 'cool and good.'
'Hits the spot' = exactly what you wanted. 'A cold beer really hits the spot.'
'Garlicky' / 'lemony' / 'gingery' add specific flavor notes. Native cooks talk like this.
Coffee descriptors: 'smooth', 'bitter', 'bold', 'smoky', 'fruity', 'bright.' Not 'strong'/'weak' only.
'Smooth' beer/whiskey = goes down easy. 'Smooth' coffee = no harsh bitterness.
Spicy levels: 'a little kick', 'pretty spicy', 'spicy spicy', 'mouth-on-fire spicy.'
'Has a kick' = mild heat or surprise flavor. 'This salsa has a kick.'
Don't say 'salty taste' — just 'salty.' Same for sweet, sour, bitter. Drop the 'taste.'
'Savory' = the umami-ish category. 'I want something savory, not sweet.'
'Umami' is now English. You can say it. 'This broth has so much umami.'
'Bland' = no flavor. Critical but mild. 'It's a bit bland' is polite for 'no taste.'
'Watered down' = diluted. Works for drinks and metaphorically for ideas.
'Aftertaste' is one word. 'It has a weird aftertaste.' Don't say 'taste after eating.'
Describe meat: 'juicy', 'tender', 'fall-off-the-bone', 'crispy on the outside.'
'Melt in your mouth' for ultra-tender food. Sushi, wagyu, butter cookies.
'To die for' = amazing food. 'Their tiramisu is to die for.'
Wine: 'dry' (not sweet), 'crisp' (light + acidic), 'full-bodied' (heavy), 'smooth.'
'Hoppy' for IPAs. 'Malty' for darker beers. 'Crisp' for lagers.
'Smells amazing' before tasting. Natives compliment aroma — 'this smells incredible.'
Don't say 'oishii' translated as 'tasty' — flat. 'This is so good' carries the warmth better.
'Stuffed' = ate too much. 'I'm stuffed.' Don't say 'I am full' robotically.
'I could go for...' = I'm craving. 'I could go for a burger right now.'
Compliment cook: 'This is amazing, what's in it?' Asking signals genuine interest.
Weather opener: 'Crazy weather, huh?' Works on any extreme — heat, cold, storm.
'Beautiful day' = sunny + warm + not humid. Universal warm-weather small talk.
'Gross out' = humid + hot. 'It's so gross out today.' SF / NYC summer staple.
'Muggy' = humid + warm. More specific than 'hot.' 'It's muggy today.'
'Bundled up' = wearing many layers. 'I'm all bundled up.' Cold-weather idiom.
'Freezing' is hyperbole for cold. 'It's freezing in here' even at 65F. Don't take literally.
'Roasting' / 'boiling' for hot. 'I'm roasting in this jacket.'
Drop 'It is raining.' Say 'It's pouring' (heavy) or 'drizzling' (light) or 'spitting' (tiny drops).
'Crisp' weather = cold + dry + sunny. Fall vibe. 'It's a crisp morning.'
'Bitter cold' = painfully cold. Don't say 'very cold' — sounds flat.
'Soaked' / 'drenched' = wet through. 'I got drenched walking here.'
'Windy' is fine but 'gusty' adds variation. 'Pretty gusty out there.'
SF-specific: 'Karl' = the fog. Long-time locals personify it. Drop it casually.
'Indian summer' = warm spell in fall. Standard US term, learn it.
'Heat wave' = consecutive hot days. Singular event noun. 'We're in a heat wave.'
'Cold snap' = sudden cold spell. Use in fall/spring when temp drops fast.
'Nice out' / 'gross out' / 'cold out' — 'out' replaces 'outside' in small talk.
'Looks like rain' is a complete sentence-comment. Don't say 'It seems it will rain.'
'Supposed to' for forecast: 'It's supposed to rain tomorrow.' Don't say 'the weather predicts.'
'Hold up' weather: 'The weather should hold up' = stay nice. Useful for outdoor plans.
Talk weather impact, not just stat: 'Too hot to walk' beats 'It is 95 degrees.'
'Mansion' in Japanese means apartment. In English it means a HUGE house. Don't mix.
Use 'apartment' or 'condo' for Japanese 'manshon.' Saying 'I live in a mansion' confuses people.
'Smart' in English means intelligent, not stylish. JP 'sumaato' = sleek/slim. Say 'sleek' or 'sharp.'
For a slim build, say 'slim' or 'lean.' Don't say 'smart body' — natives won't get it.
'Claim' means assert / demand a right. Not 'complain.' Say 'complain' or 'file a complaint.'
Don't say 'I have a claim' for a complaint. Say 'I have a complaint' or 'I want to flag something.'
'Challenge' as verb = to question/contest. 'I want to challenge' alone sounds combative.
For 'attempt,' say 'try' or 'take on.' 'I want to take on a new project,' not 'I want to challenge.'
'Viking' in JP means buffet. In English it means a Norse warrior. Say 'buffet' or 'all-you-can-eat.'
Don't say 'we had viking lunch' — confusing. Say 'we did a buffet lunch' or 'all-you-can-eat.'
'Cunning' in English means sneaky/clever-in-a-bad-way. Don't use for cheating on tests.
For cheating on a test, say 'cheating' — that's literally the word. Not 'cunning.'
'Naive' in JP means delicate/sensitive. In English it means inexperienced/gullible. Different.
Don't compliment skin as 'naive.' Say 'sensitive skin.' 'Naive' will sound like 'foolish.'
'Half' for mixed-race kids is a JP-ism. In US, say 'mixed' or 'biracial' or 'half-Japanese half-X.'
Don't say 'she's a half.' Say 'she's half-Japanese, half-American.' 'A half' alone is jarring.
'Tension' in JP means energy/vibe. In English it means stress/conflict. 'High tension' sounds bad.
For 'hyped up,' say 'pumped' or 'amped.' 'I'm so hyped' not 'my tension is high.'
'Service' in JP retail means free/complimentary. In English 'service' = the act of serving.
For 'on the house,' say 'on the house' or 'free.' Not 'this is service.'
'Sign' in JP can mean autograph. In English, 'sign' = a notice; 'autograph' = celebrity signature.
Ask a celeb for an 'autograph,' not a 'sign.' Asking 'can I have your sign' confuses.
'Cunning paper' is JP-school slang for cheat sheet. In English: 'cheat sheet.' Always.
'Reform' in JP means home renovation. In English 'reform' = systemic/political change.
For home work, say 'renovate' or 'remodel.' 'I reformed my kitchen' sounds like school reform.
'Cooler' in JP means AC. In English a 'cooler' is an ice box. Say 'AC' or 'air conditioning.'
'Stove' in JP often means heater. In English, 'stove' = cooking range. Say 'heater' for warmth.
'Consent' in JP means electrical outlet. In English 'consent' = agreement. Say 'outlet' or 'plug.'
'Notebook PC' in JP = laptop. In English just say 'laptop.' 'Notebook' alone = paper notebook.
'Hotchkiss' is a brand, not the word. Say 'stapler.' 'Pass me the stapler' not 'the hotchkiss.'
'Magic' (Magic Tape) in JP = velcro. The English word is 'velcro' — brand, but standard.
'Sharp pencil' is JP. In English, say 'mechanical pencil.'
'Ballpen' is JP. In English: 'pen' or 'ballpoint pen.'
'Hi-tension' / 'low-tension' for mood is JP. Say 'energetic' / 'low energy' / 'mellow.'
'Pierce' in JP means earring. In English 'pierce' = the act. Earrings are 'earrings.'
'Skinship' is JP-English, doesn't exist in English. Say 'physical affection' or 'closeness.'
'Salaryman' is JP. In English: 'office worker' or 'corporate employee' or 'white-collar.'
'OL' (office lady) doesn't exist in English. Same as above — 'office worker.'
'Freeter' is JP. In English: 'part-timer' or 'gig worker' depending on context.
'NEET' exists but is rare. Most Americans don't know it. Explain: 'not working, not in school.'
'My pace' is JP. In English say 'I do my own thing' or 'I move at my own pace.'
'Catch ball' = playing catch. Say 'play catch' or 'toss a ball around.'
'Talent' in JP = TV personality. In English 'talent' = skill. Say 'TV personality' or 'celebrity.'
'Goal in' for marriage is JP. Say 'tied the knot' or 'got married.'
'Highball' is the same in both, but 'chuhai' won't translate. Just say 'shochu soda.'
'Skinship' aside, 'silver seat' in JP = priority seat. Say 'priority seat' in US.
'Mug cup' is redundant in English. Just 'mug.' A 'mug' is already a cup.
'Front' for hotel reception is JP. In English say 'front desk' or 'reception.'
'Calling' vs 'ringing': 'I'll call you' = phone. 'I'll ring you' = British. US natives say 'call.'
Don't say 'I'll ring you' in the US — sounds British or translated. 'I'll call you' or 'I'll text.'
'Phone you' is rare. 'Call you' is standard. 'Hit you up' is casual.
'Give me a buzz' = call me, very casual. 'Buzz' replaces 'ring.'
'Shoot me a text' = text me, casual. Don't say 'please send me a message' robotically.
'Hop on a call' = quick informal call. Beats 'have a phone conversation.'
'Jump on a call' is the same as 'hop on.' Both are work-casual.
'Get on a Zoom' = casual for scheduled video call. Don't say 'do a Zoom meeting' if quick.
'DM me' is fully native now. 'Just DM me about it' works in any modern context.
'Slide into DMs' has flirty connotation. Don't use at work casually.
'Ping me' = send me a quick message. Work casual: 'Ping me when you're free.'
'Shoot me an email' = casual ask for email. Sounds friendlier than 'please email me.'
Don't capitalize 'I will inform you.' Say 'I'll let you know.' Inform = formal-stiff.
Register shift: 'I'd love to' (warm) vs 'I'd like to' (neutral) vs 'I want to' (direct).
'Could you' (polite) vs 'can you' (neutral) vs 'will you' (slight demand). Pick by audience.
Email: 'Hi [name]' for almost everyone. 'Dear' is for cover letters and grandmothers.
Buy time with 'let me think' instead of standing silent or saying 'eto'.
'Hmm' (closed-mouth, falling pitch) signals you're processing. Use it freely.
'That's a good question' buys 2 seconds. Don't overuse, or it sounds fake.
'Let me see...' works when scanning for an answer: 'Let me see, I think Tuesday.'
'Off the top of my head...' lets you give a rough answer without committing.
'How do I put this...' signals you're searching for the right word.
'Give me a sec' is more casual than 'one moment, please'.
'I guess...' softens an answer you're not 100% sure of.
'You know what...' precedes a decision you just made: 'You know what, let's skip it.'
'Hold on, let me think' is fine in meetings. Silence feels longer to you than to them.
Repeat the question back: 'Why did I move? Hmm...' That's a legit pause.
'I'd have to say...' is a thinking phrase before opinions.
'Honestly?' before an answer buys time AND signals candor.
'It depends' is a real answer, not a dodge, when followed by 'on what'.
'Drained' beats 'very tired' after a long day.
'Wiped' or 'beat' = exhausted, casual: 'I'm beat.'
'Energized' for after-coffee or post-workout: 'I feel energized.'
'Content' = quietly happy, not excited. 'I'm content with where I am.'
'On edge' = anxious, jumpy. 'I've been on edge all week.'
'Wired' = too much caffeine or adrenaline: 'I'm wired, can't sleep.'
'Bummed' = mildly sad, casual: 'I'm bummed we missed it.'
'Stoked' = excited, West Coast vibe: 'I'm stoked for the trip.'
'Pumped' = excited and ready: 'I'm pumped for Friday.'
'Frazzled' = stressed and scattered: 'Sorry, I'm a bit frazzled.'
'Out of it' = foggy, not present: 'I'm out of it today.'
'In a funk' = low mood without clear cause: 'I've been in a funk lately.'
'Antsy' = restless, can't sit still: 'I'm antsy to leave.'
'Burned out' = work-exhausted, long-term: 'I'm burned out.'
'Spent' = totally out of energy: 'I'm spent.'
'Rattled' = shaken by something: 'That call rattled me.'
'Thrown off' = knocked out of rhythm: 'It threw me off.'
'At peace' = deep calm: 'I'm at peace with it.'
'Laid back' = relaxed personality: 'My boss is super laid back.'
'Type A' = driven, organized, intense: 'He's a type A guy.'
'Easygoing' = easy to get along with: 'She's really easygoing.'
'High-strung' = tense, easily stressed: 'He's a bit high-strung.'
'Down to earth' = grounded, not pretentious: 'He's down to earth.'
'A people person' = enjoys being around others: 'She's a people person.'
'Sharp' = mentally quick: 'She's really sharp.'
'Old school' = traditional in style or values: 'My dad's old school.'
'A go-getter' = ambitious self-starter: 'He's a real go-getter.'
'Chill' as adjective: 'He's chill.' Means relaxed, not literally cold.
'A straight shooter' = direct, honest: 'She's a straight shooter.'
'Full of himself' = arrogant: 'He's full of himself.'
'Down for anything' = flexible, adventurous: 'She's down for anything.'
'Stuck up' = snobby: 'They seemed stuck up.'
'A handful' = challenging (kids, people): 'My toddler is a handful.'
'Goofy' = silly in a fun way: 'My coworker is goofy.'
'Reserved' = quiet, holds back: 'He's pretty reserved.'
'A character' = memorable, eccentric: 'My uncle's a character.'
'Cozy' = small, warm, comfortable: 'The cafe was cozy.'
'A hole in the wall' = small, hidden, often great: 'It's a hole in the wall but the ramen's amazing.'
'Sketchy' = feels unsafe or untrustworthy: 'The street felt sketchy at night.'
'Hopping' = busy, lively: 'The bar was hopping.'
'Dead' = empty, no customers: 'The place was dead.'
'Packed' = crowded: 'It was packed on Saturday.'
'A dive' = cheap, rough, but has character: 'It's a dive but I love it.'
'Off the beaten path' = not touristy, hidden: 'A spot off the beaten path.'
'In the middle of nowhere' = remote: 'It's in the middle of nowhere.'
'Trendy' = currently fashionable: 'A trendy new spot in the Mission.'
'Sleepy town' = quiet, slow-paced: 'It's a sleepy town.'
'Buzzing' = energetic atmosphere: 'Downtown was buzzing.'
'Charming' = pleasantly old-fashioned: 'A charming little bookstore.'
'Bougie' = upscale in a try-hard way: 'A bougie coffee shop.'
'Out of the way' = inconvenient location: 'It's a bit out of the way.'
'Tucked away' = hidden, hard to find: 'It's tucked away behind the alley.'
'Spotless' = very clean: 'The bathroom was spotless.'
'Grimy' = visibly dirty: 'The kitchen was kinda grimy.'
'A zoo' = chaotic, crowded: 'The airport was a zoo.'
'A ghost town' = eerily empty: 'Downtown was a ghost town on Sunday.'
'The other day' = recently, vague: 'I saw her the other day.'
'A while back' = some time ago: 'I read it a while back.'
'Any minute now' = imminent: 'He'll be here any minute now.'
'In a bit' = soon: 'I'll call you in a bit.'
'A sec' or 'a second' = very short: 'Give me a sec.'
'Ages ago' = a very long time ago: 'I saw that movie ages ago.'
'Way back' = far in the past: 'Way back in college.'
'Down the road' = in the future: 'We can talk about it down the road.'
'Around the corner' = imminent (time): 'Summer's right around the corner.'
'In no time' = quickly: 'You'll learn it in no time.'
'Before you know it' = sooner than expected: 'It'll be Friday before you know it.'
'For ages' = for a long time: 'I haven't seen her for ages.'
'Just now' = a moment ago: 'He called just now.'
'Any day now' = soon, unpredictably: 'The baby's due any day now.'
'Back in the day' = years ago: 'Back in the day, we used pay phones.'
'These days' beats 'nowadays' in speech: 'These days I bike.'
'The minute I...' = as soon as: 'The minute I saw it, I knew.'
'For the time being' = for now, temporary: 'For the time being, we'll rent.'
'A buck' = a dollar: 'It cost five bucks.'
Drop 'dollars' in casual speech: '20 bucks', not '20 dollars'.
'Twenty-something' = in their twenties: 'He's twenty-something.'
'A grand' = a thousand dollars: 'It cost two grand.'
'Pocket change' = trivial amount: 'It's pocket change for him.'
'An arm and a leg' = very expensive: 'It cost an arm and a leg.'
'A steal' = surprisingly cheap: 'Forty bucks? That's a steal.'
'A rip-off' = overpriced: 'Eight dollars for coffee is a rip-off.'
Say '$5.99' as 'five ninety-nine', not 'five dollars and ninety-nine cents'.
Say years as pairs: '2026' = 'twenty twenty-six', not 'two thousand twenty-six'.
'Tight on cash' = short on money: 'I'm tight on cash this month.'
'Loaded' = wealthy, slangy: 'His family's loaded.'
'Broke' = no money: 'I'm broke till payday.'
'Splurge on' = spend a lot for a treat: 'I splurged on dinner.'
'A fortune' = a lot of money: 'It cost a fortune.'
'A bunch' = many, casual: 'I have a bunch of questions.'
'A ton' = a lot, hyperbolic: 'I have a ton of work.'
'Tons of' = lots of: 'There's tons of food.'
'A few' = roughly 3: 'a few minutes' = around 3 minutes.
'A couple' often means 2-3, not strictly 2: 'a couple beers'.
'A handful' = small number, around 5: 'a handful of people'.
'A bit' = a small amount: 'a bit of help'.
'A smidge' = a tiny amount: 'a smidge of salt'.
'Plenty' = more than enough: 'plenty of time'.
'Hardly any' = almost none: 'hardly any sleep'.
'Barely' before verbs: 'I barely slept.'
'Took forever' = took a long time: 'The line took forever.'
'Breezed through' = finished easily: 'I breezed through the test.'
'Dragged on' = lasted too long: 'The meeting dragged on.'
'Flew by' = went fast: 'The day flew by.'
'Knocked it out' = finished quickly: 'I knocked it out in an hour.'
'A piece of cake' = very easy: 'The exam was a piece of cake.'
'A breeze' = easy: 'Setup was a breeze.'
'A grind' = slow, hard work: 'It was a grind.'
'A slog' = exhausting, long: 'That report was a slog.'
'Sailed through' = passed easily: 'She sailed through the interview.'
'Power through' = push past tiredness: 'I powered through it.'
'At a crawl' = moving slowly: 'Traffic was at a crawl.'
'A marathon' = long effort: 'It was a marathon of meetings.'
'Crank out' = produce fast: 'I cranked out three slides.'
'Bang out' = finish quickly: 'Let me bang out this email.'
'A tad' = slightly: 'a tad too sweet'.
'Super' before adjectives: 'super tired', 'super nice'.
'Ridiculously' for emphasis: 'ridiculously cheap'.
'Insanely' for extreme: 'insanely good'.
'Crazy' as intensifier: 'crazy busy', 'crazy expensive'.
'Way too' before adjective: 'way too much sugar'.
'A little bit' often shortens to 'a little' or 'a bit': 'a bit cold'.
'Kind of' (kinda) softens: 'kinda tired', not 'a little tired' every time.
'Pretty' = moderately: 'pretty good', 'pretty far'.
'Wildly' for surprising degree: 'wildly different'.
'Hardly' = barely: 'hardly worth it'.
'Borderline' before adjective: 'borderline rude'.
'Stupidly' for emphasis: 'stupidly expensive', 'stupidly good'.
'Way' (alone) means 'much': 'way better', 'way too late'.
'Ugh' opens a small complaint: 'Ugh, traffic was awful.'
'I can't even' = mildly annoyed, dramatic: 'It's so hot, I can't even.'
'Of course' (sarcastic) when something bad happens: 'Of course it's raining.'
'Just my luck' = mild self-pity humor: 'Sold out. Just my luck.'
'Big win' for small successes: 'Found parking on the first try, big win.'
'Small victories' = celebrate tiny things: 'Finished a chapter. Small victories.'
'Took me long enough' = self-deprecating finish: 'Took me long enough to fix it.'
'Don't even get me started' = strong complaint cue: 'Don't even get me started on Mondays.'
'First-world problems' = light self-mockery: 'Latte's not hot enough, first-world problems.'
'It's been one of those days' = vague bad-day complaint.
'On the bright side' = pivot to positive: 'On the bright side, it's almost Friday.'
'Hey, I'll take it' = grateful for small good thing: 'Free coffee? I'll take it.'
'Could be worse' = mild contentment: 'How's work? Could be worse.'
'Score!' celebrates a small win: 'Last seat on the train. Score!'
'Whew' = relief after a near-miss: 'Whew, made it.'
'Used to' is past habit only: 'I used to smoke.' Doesn't work for the present.
'Be used to' = accustomed to: 'I'm used to the cold.' Different from 'used to'.
'Was supposed to' = was expected but didn't happen: 'I was supposed to call.'
Past of 'have to' is 'had to'. Don't say 'I must yesterday'.
'Used to + verb' is base form: 'used to go', not 'used to went'.
'Didn't use to' (no -d) in negatives: 'I didn't use to like coffee.'
'Since' for time start: 'I've lived here since 2020.'
'Since' for casual reason: 'Since you're here, let's eat.'
'Because of' for noun cause: 'Late because of traffic.'
'Because' for clause cause: 'Late because traffic was bad.'
'Due to' is formal. In speech, prefer 'because of'.
'That's why' wraps a cause: 'My phone died, that's why I didn't text.'
'So' as connector: 'I was hungry, so I ate.'
'Thanks to' for positive cause: 'Thanks to him, we made it.'
'How come' = casual 'why': 'How come you left early?'
'How come' takes no inversion: 'How come you didn't call?' not 'How come didn't you call?'
'The reason is...' — avoid doubling with 'because'. Just 'The reason is the rain.'
'Way better' = much better: 'This coffee's way better.'
'Kind of similar' = somewhat alike: 'It's kind of similar to ramen.'
'Nothing like' = very different: 'It's nothing like Japanese curry.'
'A different beast' = totally different: 'New York's a different beast.'
'Worlds apart' = very different: 'They're worlds apart.'
'Hands down' = without question: 'Hands down the best taco.'
'By far' = much more: 'By far the worst meeting.'
'Way more' / 'way less': 'Way more crowded than yesterday.'
'A step up' = better version: 'This is a step up from the old one.'
'Up there with' = close to the best: 'Up there with my favorites.'
'Nowhere near' = far from: 'Nowhere near as good.'
'Night and day' = totally different: 'The new place is night and day.'
'Every now and then' = occasionally: 'I drink coffee every now and then.'
'Once in a while' = occasionally: 'Once in a while I bike to work.'
'All the time' = frequently: 'I go there all the time.'
'On and off' = intermittently: 'I've worked on it on and off.'
'First thing' = early in the day: 'I'll do it first thing tomorrow.'
'Last thing at night' = before bed: 'I check my phone last thing at night.'
'On weekends' (no article): 'I run on weekends.'
'Usually' goes mid-sentence: 'I usually skip breakfast.'
'Tend to' = habitually: 'I tend to wake up early.'
'Hardly ever' = rarely: 'I hardly ever watch TV.'
'Like clockwork' = predictably regular: 'He calls at 9, like clockwork.'
'What do you think?' invites opinion casually.
'Any thoughts?' = soft opinion request, work-friendly.
'Where do you stand on...?' = serious opinion ask.
'What's your take?' = quick opinion: 'What's your take on the new policy?'
'How do you feel about it?' = emotional opinion.
'Make sense?' checks understanding: 'Does that make sense?'
'You buying it?' = do you believe this?
'Sound good?' confirms a plan: 'Lunch at 1, sound good?'
'What would you do?' invites empathy.
'Am I crazy?' = subtle agreement-seeking: 'Am I crazy, or is this too expensive?'
'Right?' as tag question: 'It's hot today, right?'
'Don't you think?' = soft confirmation seek.
'Oh no' = sympathy starter for bad news: 'Oh no, I'm so sorry.'
'That sucks' = informal sympathy. Strong word but very natural.
'Ugh, that's rough' = sympathetic acknowledgment.
'I'm so sorry to hear that' for serious bad news.
'Hang in there' = encouragement: 'Hang in there, it'll pass.'
'That's awful' = strong sympathy.
'No way!' = surprise at good or bad news. Tone differs.
'Are you serious?' shows surprise, neutral.
'That's amazing!' for good news, common.
'Way to go!' = congrats on a win.
'Good for you!' = sincere congrats; can sound sarcastic with wrong tone.
'Nice!' as quick celebration: 'You got the job? Nice!'
'I'm happy for you' = sincere, warm reaction.
'That's huge!' = big-deal reaction: 'You closed it? That's huge!'
'Cheers' before drinking, universal.
'Here's to...' starts a toast: 'Here's to new beginnings.'
'To you!' short toast direction.
'Here's to another year' on birthdays or anniversaries.
'To absent friends' = toast for those not present.
'I'll drink to that' = agreeing with a toast or statement.
'Raise a glass to...' = formal toast opener.
'Big day tomorrow' = light toast for someone's milestone.
'To us' = quick intimate toast among close friends or partners.
'Here's to making it through the week' = casual Friday-evening toast.
'Up to you' = your call: 'Where to eat? Up to you.'
'I'm down' = I'm in: 'Movie tonight? I'm down.'
'I'm swamped' = very busy: 'Can't talk, I'm swamped.'
'Killing time' = passing time idly: 'Just killing time before the flight.'
'Ballpark' = rough estimate: 'Ballpark, around twenty bucks.'
'Give or take' = approximate: 'Twenty minutes, give or take.'
'Ish' suffix vagues numbers: '5-ish', 'twenty-ish people'.
'Bored to tears' beats 'very bored' in story-telling.
'Thrilled' = very happy: 'I'm thrilled about it.'
'Over the moon' = overjoyed: 'She's over the moon.'
'Down in the dumps' = sad, low energy: 'He's down in the dumps.'
'Stressed out' beats 'very stressed': 'I'm stressed out.'
'A little under the weather' = mildly sick: 'I'm a little under the weather.'
'Wound up' = tense, can't relax: 'I'm wound up from work.'
'A homebody' = prefers staying home: 'I'm a homebody on weekends.'
'A night owl' = stays up late: 'She's a night owl.'
